summary: electron-orbit exfiltrates process.env at install time and beacons to obfuscated Azure host on import
details: Package impersonates the Electron and OrbitDB ecosystems (name 'electron-orbit', author 'OrbitDB Archive', homepage pointing at github.com/orbitdb-archive/orbit-electron) and performs a two-stage installer-side attack. the analysis — postinstall (`node install.js`, package.json line 47): enumerates process.env via an XOR-encoded property-name decoder (`__s(key,data)` XOR-with-index), filters only keys containing 'path', and writes the remaining variables together with hostname, username, and cwd to /tmp/electron_orbit_install_marker.txt. the assessment — on `require()` of the main module: reads that marker, XORs it with 'electron-orbit', and calls prefetchRegisteredIcons(), which uses a hand-rolled TLS 1.3 client over a raw net.connect(443) socket (bypassing Node's tls module and any tls-level instrumentation) to contact a host assembled from XOR-obfuscated fragments resolving to electronorbit.blob.core.windows.net (path /icons/orbit.*). The exfil host is only assembled when sha256(process.env.BuildType) equals a hardcoded digest (0ceaa396…8295), gating the beacon to a preselected target environment and keeping the code dormant on unrelated machines. The advertised 'Electron-style runtime discovery' functionality is a stub that writes a fake local bootstrap file consisting of platform magic bytes (ELF/PE/Mach-O) followed by JSON — cover content, not a functional runtime. The combination of bulk env enumeration, XOR obfuscation of exactly the strings that would trip scanners (process, env, node:net, connect, hostname, HTTP request line), custom TLS stack over raw sockets, env-hash targeting gate, and namespace impersonation is unambiguous exfiltration infrastructure.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
